How to use the `git filter-branch` command
=============================================

The `git filter-branch` command is a powerful tool that allows you to modify the history of a Git repository. It can be used to re-write the commit history of a branch, squash multiple commits into a single commit, or even remove commits entirely. In this guide, we will go over the basics of how to use the `git filter-branch` command, as well as some advanced techniques for using it effectively.

Basic Usage
-------------

The basic syntax of the `git filter-branch` command is as follows:
```
git filter-branch <branch-name> -- <commit-range>
```
Here, `<branch-name>` is the name of the branch you want to modify, and `<commit-range>` is the range of commits you want to modify. For example:
```
git filter-branch my-branch -- 1234..4567
```
This command will modify the `my-branch` branch, starting from the 1234th commit and ending at the 4567th commit.

How to use the `git` command in Linux
============================================

The `git` command is a powerful tool for managing version control in Linux. Here are some basic commands and concepts you should know to get started with `git`:

1. Initialize a new Git repository:
```
git init
```
This command creates a new Git repository in the current directory.

2. Add files to the repository:
```
git add <file name>
```
This command adds the specified file to the Git repository. You can also use `git add .` to add all files in the current directory and subdirectories.

3. Commit changes:
```
git commit -m "commit message"
```
This command commits the changes you've made to the repository with a specific message.

4. View the commit history:
```
git log
```
This command shows you a list of all commits made to the repository, along with the author and commit message.

5. View the current branch:
```
git branch
```
